copy.title: Spurs steal Game 3. Antonelli dominates Monaco. World Cup starts Thursday.
copy.keyTakeaway: Spurs force Game 4 after stealing one in New York; tech stocks rally on chip strength while broader market stays flat; World Cup kicks off in two days.

todaysHits.sports: Spurs refuse to go away. Knicks up 2-1 heading home.
todaysHits.golf: Poston holds lead at Memorial Tournament, -12.
todaysHits.f1: Antonelli wins Monaco for Mercedes. Hamilton second in Ferrari.
todaysHits.worldcup: World Cup 2026 opens Thursday. Group stage begins.
todaysHits.culture: Scott Rudin wins Tony after bullying exit. Late-night roasts CBS news drama.

lead.headline: Wembanyama and Brunson duel in Spurs' must-win game three.
lead.whatHappened: Victor Wembanyama dropped 32 points and ran the offense with 6 assists while Jalen Brunson matched him point-for-point, but San Antonio's depth—Stephon Castle added 23 and Dylan Harper grabbed 9 boards—was enough to steal Game 3 at home. New York still leads the series 2-1, but the Spurs just proved they can win without needing a blowout.
lead.whyBullet1: The Knicks have an answer for Wembanyama's individual dominance in OG Anunoby (28 points), but they can't match San Antonio's secondary scoring when Castle and Harper get rolling.
lead.whyBullet2: Game 4 is tomorrow in New York, which means the Spurs have to survive hostile territory or they're headed home down 3-1 with no real margin for error.
lead.whatToSay: Brunson and Wembanyama basically canceled each other out, so this one came down to Josh Hart and Dylan Harper—and Harper's the one who showed up on the glass.

markets.mood: Tech stocks closed mixed Monday as the Mag Seven fractured — Tesla surged 4.6% while Apple and Microsoft dropped, leaving the S&P 500 up just 0.2% and the Nasdaq up 1.6%.
markets.whyBullet1: The divergence reflects real disagreement about which AI winners will actually print money. Tesla's jump came on something specific; Apple and Microsoft's slides suggest traders are rotating away from the safest bets into riskier plays.
markets.whyBullet2: The 10-year Treasury yield jumped 0.4% to close the week up 1.7% — watch for CPI data Thursday to see if inflation fears are back. That's the real story for the next three days.
markets.bringUp: Dell closed up 1.6% Monday while its bigger competitors stumbled. That tells you some traders still think there's value in the unsexy infrastructure play that benefits from AI capex, regardless of which chip maker wins the narrative.

golf.headline: Poston edges Gerard in playoff thriller at Muirfield Village
golf.whyCare1: Memorial is the PGA Tour's de facto regular-season championship — winner gets FedEx Cup points and serious credibility heading into summer.
golf.whyCare2: Muirfield Village always plays brutal; -12 winning score shows how tough Jack Nicklaus designed this place to be.
golf.watchFor: Whether Poston can convert this momentum into top-10 finishes at the majors coming up — he's got the game but needs consistency on the biggest stages.
golf.whatToSay: Poston finally broke through at a course where the best players in the world show up. That matters more than the score.

f1.headline: Antonelli wins Monaco, extends championship lead to 66 points
f1.whyCare1: Antonelli took the most prestigious street circuit in racing with Hamilton and Red Bull right there, which means Mercedes has the car and the driver to hold off everything else.
f1.whyCare2: With 5 wins already this season and 156 points, Antonelli's gap to second place is massive — Monaco just made it harder for anyone chasing to close the gap.
f1.watchFor: Whether Red Bull can find speed on the remaining street circuits, since Hadjar's P3 here shows they're not where they need to be in tight quarters.
f1.whatToSay: Antonelli's just running a different race than everyone else right now — five wins before summer break is the kind of pace that wins championships.

culture.topic: Scott Pelley fired for being too cool
culture.whatHappened: CBS News let Scott Pelley go, and John Oliver joked on air that the network fired him for the crime of being too cool in a meeting.
culture.whyItMatters: Pelley was one of the last old-school anchor voices — solid, trustworthy, not trying too hard. If networks are dumping that, they're chasing something else entirely.
culture.whatToSay: Yeah, I saw that. Oliver's right though — nobody gets fired for being too good at the job anymore.

culture.topic: Scott Rudin wins Tony after leaving industry
culture.whatHappened: Scott Rudin, the Hollywood producer who stepped back from the industry after bullying allegations came out, just won a Tony Award.
culture.whyItMatters: It raises the question of what accountability actually looks like in entertainment — the guy left, stayed gone, and now he's getting awards again. That's the cycle people talk about.
culture.whatToSay: Rudin won a Tony. Yeah, that one's complicated.

culture.whatHappened: Steven Soderbergh's spy thriller: Michael Fassbender and Cate Blanchett as intelligence agents who are married to each other — until one of them is suspected of being a mole.

copy.finalSharpTake: The Spurs stole Game 3 at Madison Square Garden and suddenly the Knicks' Finals lead doesn't feel comfortable anymore. San Antonio plays the kind of defense that makes New York's role players disappear—they can't live off role players in Game 4. This series just flipped. The Knicks still control it, but they're not running away, and that's the problem when you face a team this disciplined.

theTake.office: The Spurs aren't dead yet—they stole Game 3 on the road and the Knicks' balanced attack suddenly looks vulnerable in a closeout situation.
theTake.bar: New York's choking this away. Up 2-1 at home and couldn't put away San Antonio without Wembanyama having to carry them. They lose Game 4, it's over.
